CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and the main contributions (four linguistic regularizers for LSTM/Bi-LSTM) are stated early and developed coherently through method and experiments; overall writing quality is good. Notation and presentation of the regularizers are largely consistent and understandable, and figures/tables/examples effectively illustrate the approach and results on MR and SST. However, important experimental details that affect clarity and reproducibility are omitted (no hyperparameter reporting, no code mentioned), and the decision to omit negation scope resolution—while stated—would benefit from clearer motivation and discussion of practical implications. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (LR-LSTM with four linguistic regularizers) are stated early and developed coherently through method and experiments. Notation and methodological descriptions appear clear enough to follow, and the writing quality is reported as good with thorough related work coverage. Presentation could be polished by reporting hyperparameters/code for reproducibility and by giving clearer indication of figures/tables effectiveness (e.g., more qualitative examples/visualizations to show the linguistic behaviors claimed). From an applied-linguistics perspective the omission of negation-scope modeling is acknowledged, which helps clarity, but more implementation details would improve practical usability.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized and the main contributions (four linguistic regularizers for LSTM/Bi-LSTM) are stated early and developed coherently through method and experiments; overall writing quality is good. Notation and presentation of the regularizers are largely consistent and understandable, and figures/tables/examples effectively illustrate the approach and results on MR and SST. However, important experimental details that affect clarity and reproducibility are omitted (no hyperparameter reporting, no code mentioned), and the decision to omit negation scope resolution—while stated—would benefit from clearer motivation and discussion of practical implications.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (LR-LSTM with four linguistic regularizers) are stated early and developed coherently through method and experiments. Notation and methodological descriptions appear clear enough to follow, and the writing quality is reported as good with thorough related work coverage. Presentation could be polished by reporting hyperparameters/code for reproducibility and by giving clearer indication of figures/tables effectiveness (e.g., more qualitative examples/visualizations to show the linguistic behaviors claimed). From an applied-linguistics perspective the omission of negation-scope modeling is acknowledged, which helps clarity, but more implementation details would improve practical usability.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper proposes a specific way to inject linguistic knowledge into LSTM classifiers via four KL-divergence based regularizers (NSR, SR, NR, IR) and word-specific transformation matrices — a concrete but incremental augmentation to standard LSTM/Bi-LSTM architectures. The problem (sentence-level sentiment classification) is well-studied, and prior work already integrates lexicons or context-sensitive lexica (e.g., NCSL); the contribution is a practical regularization design rather than a fundamentally new problem formulation or theoretical advance. From a theoretical-purist perspective the techniques are a non-trivial engineering extension but would not surprise an expert, since they largely combine known ideas (lexicon constraints, distribution-shifting, per-word transforms) without formal guarantees or novel optimization/learning theory. | B(3): The paper proposes a concrete, practical way to inject linguistic priors (sentiment lexicons, negation, intensity) into LSTM classifiers via four KL-divergence based regularizers and word-specific transformation matrices, which is a useful operationalization not widely standardized. However, the overall idea of using lexicons and modeling negation/intensification with learned transformations builds on well-known prior directions (lexicon-informed models, transformation-based negation modeling, and regularization), so the contribution feels incremental rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. The authors do clearly differentiate their specific regularizers and show they work with sentence-level supervision on MR and SST, making the work a notable, pragmatic extension rather than a breakthrough.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a specific way to inject linguistic knowledge into LSTM classifiers via four KL-divergence based regularizers (NSR, SR, NR, IR) and word-specific transformation matrices — a concrete but incremental augmentation to standard LSTM/Bi-LSTM architectures. The problem (sentence-level sentiment classification) is well-studied, and prior work already integrates lexicons or context-sensitive lexica (e.g., NCSL); the contribution is a practical regularization design rather than a fundamentally new problem formulation or theoretical advance. From a theoretical-purist perspective the techniques are a non-trivial engineering extension but would not surprise an expert, since they largely combine known ideas (lexicon constraints, distribution-shifting, per-word transforms) without formal guarantees or novel optimization/learning theory.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a concrete, practical way to inject linguistic priors (sentiment lexicons, negation, intensity) into LSTM classifiers via four KL-divergence based regularizers and word-specific transformation matrices, which is a useful operationalization not widely standardized. However, the overall idea of using lexicons and modeling negation/intensification with learned transformations builds on well-known prior directions (lexicon-informed models, transformation-based negation modeling, and regularization), so the contribution feels incremental rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. The authors do clearly differentiate their specific regularizers and show they work with sentence-level supervision on MR and SST, making the work a notable, pragmatic extension rather than a breakthrough.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper tackles a relevant applied problem—improving sentence-level sentiment classification by injecting linguistic knowledge—which is of interest to the community and shows empirical gains on MR and SST. However, the contribution is incremental: it relies on external sentiment lexicons and heuristic handling of negation/intensity (scope resolution explicitly omitted), limiting generality and theoretical insight. The proposed regularizers could spur follow-up work on linguistically informed losses, but without stronger theoretical guarantees, broad ablation evidence, or demonstration of generalization beyond two benchmark datasets, the likely impact is moderate rather than transformative. | B(3): This paper tackles a practically important problem—bringing explicit linguistic knowledge (lexicons, negation, intensity) into end-to-end LSTM sentence classifiers—and demonstrates empirical gains on standard benchmarks (MR, SST) while learning from sentence-level labels alone. From my perspective as an applied linguistically-minded reviewer, the proposed KL-based regularizers and word-specific transformations are pragmatically appealing and interpretable, and they provide a clear, implementable direction for integrating linguistic priors without complex annotation. However, the scope is incremental rather than transformative (negation scope is omitted and gains appear limited by prevalence of phenomena), so I view the work as a useful, moderate-impact contribution that should spur follow-up work but not radically change the field.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles a relevant applied problem—improving sentence-level sentiment classification by injecting linguistic knowledge—which is of interest to the community and shows empirical gains on MR and SST. However, the contribution is incremental: it relies on external sentiment lexicons and heuristic handling of negation/intensity (scope resolution explicitly omitted), limiting generality and theoretical insight. The proposed regularizers could spur follow-up work on linguistically informed losses, but without stronger theoretical guarantees, broad ablation evidence, or demonstration of generalization beyond two benchmark datasets, the likely impact is moderate rather than transformative.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles a practically important problem—bringing explicit linguistic knowledge (lexicons, negation, intensity) into end-to-end LSTM sentence classifiers—and demonstrates empirical gains on standard benchmarks (MR, SST) while learning from sentence-level labels alone. From my perspective as an applied linguistically-minded reviewer, the proposed KL-based regularizers and word-specific transformations are pragmatically appealing and interpretable, and they provide a clear, implementable direction for integrating linguistic priors without complex annotation. However, the scope is incremental rather than transformative (negation scope is omitted and gains appear limited by prevalence of phenomena), so I view the work as a useful, moderate-impact contribution that should spur follow-up work but not radically change the field.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes clear claims and shows empirical gains with a plausible KL-based regularization approach, but they also identify the same methodological shortcomings—missing hyperparameter details, lack of multiple runs/significance testing, incomplete ablation reporting, and no released code. These gaps weaken confidence in the robustness and reproducibility of the results; while the approach is promising and internally consistent, the evidence is not yet rigorous enough to warrant a higher soundness score. Therefore a score of 3 reflects useful contributions tempered by important experimental and reporting deficiencies.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear, testable claims and provides experimental accuracy gains on MR and SST, but from the summary I see important technical shortcomings: the novel KL-based linguistic regularizers lack formal justification (no optimization/convexity/gradient behavior analysis) and hyperparameters for the margin/regularizer strengths are not reported. The experimental methodology appears incomplete—no mention of multiple runs, variance or significance testing, and it is unclear whether each regularizer was ablated independently to isolate effects. Given these methodological gaps and unsupported assumptions about scope handling and generalization of lexicon-driven matrices, the empirical improvements are suggestive but not yet rigorously established.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states its claims clearly and supports them with experiments showing accuracy gains on MR and SST when adding the four linguistic regularizers to LSTM/Bi-LSTM, and provides qualitative analyses that the model captures sentiment/negation/intensity roles. The mathematical formulation (KL-based regularizers, shifting distributions, word-specific transforms) is plausible and appears internally consistent given the summary, though no complex proofs are required. Experimental methodology is generally sound with appropriate baselines, but the absence of reported hyperparameters, statistical significance testing, code release, and the acknowledged omission of negation scope resolution are minor gaps that limit reproducibility and reduce confidence in generality. Overall the conclusions are justified by presented results, but practical deployment and wider applicability would benefit from additional experimental rigor and released resources.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper’s topic and audience are a good fit for NLP/ML venues and that integrating linguistic priors with LSTMs is a relevant contribution. I score it a 4 because, while the approach and evaluation on standard benchmarks make it appropriate and of interest, the reported lack of detailed hyperparameters, multiple runs/statistical testing, and thorough ablation reduce confidence in it meeting the highest methodological standards for a top-tier publication.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s topic (sentiment classification with linguistically motivated regularizers) is squarely within NLP/ML conference scope and the audience (sentence-level classification, LSTM-based models) is appropriate. However, from the provided summary I see reliance on external linguistic resources (sentiment lexicons) without analysis of generalization, limited reporting of hyperparameters and no mention of multiple runs/statistical significance or comprehensive ablation isolating each regularizer — all concerns for a top venue given my methodological standards. Thus it is borderline appropriate: relevant and of interest, but lacking the rigorous ablation, significance testing, and hyperparameter reporting I would expect for a strong fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues: it proposes a linguistically informed augmentation to LSTM classifiers that integrates sentiment lexicons, negation and intensity modeling—exactly the kind of interpretable, applied modeling contribution valued by the community. The evaluation on standard benchmarks (MR, SST) with comparisons to strong baselines and emphasis on learning from sentence-level annotations matches expected methodological standards, and the work is well positioned for researchers interested in combining linguistic priors with neural models. While code and hyperparameter details are not mentioned in the summary, the approach and analysis target the right audience and research questions for a top-tier venue.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is well-motivated and compares to reasonable baselines on standard datasets but falls short on experimental rigor: experiments are limited to MR and SST, ablations/sensitivity analyses for the four regularizers are missing, and code/hyperparameters are not provided which hurts reproducibility. Given these consistent concerns but some demonstrated empirical gains and thorough related work, a mid-level score reflects that the contribution is promising but under-supported by the current experimental evaluation.

reviewer_a.justification: Experiments are shallow: only MR and SST are used and reported improvements lack evidence of multiple runs or statistical significance (single-run gains <1% would be inconclusive under my standards). Critical ablations are missing — the paper introduces four distinct regularizers (NSR, SR, NR, IR) but the summary does not report isolated evaluations or sensitivity analyses for each component or their hyperparameters. Related work is described as thorough, and the authors acknowledge limitations (no negation scope, low prevalence of negation/intensity), but reproducibility is weak because hyperparameters and code are not reported. These omissions make the experimental support insufficient to justify the novel regularizers.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates LR-LSTM and LR-Bi-LSTM on standard benchmarks (MR, SST) and compares to a broad set of baselines, showing accuracy gains and reporting sensible qualitative observations about negation/intensity prevalence. However, the summary does not mention explicit ablation studies or sensitivity analyses for each regularizer and hyperparameter choices, and code/hyperparameters are not provided which weakens reproducibility. Related work appears thoroughly covered and the authors acknowledge key limitations (negation scope, low prevalence effects), but the experimental analysis is adequate rather than fully comprehensive for deployment-focused, linguistically informed models.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper compares against a broad, appropriate set of baselines on standard datasets and situates itself well relative to related work. However, Reviewer A rightly highlights missing experimental details (hyperparameters, code) and no reporting of multiple runs or significance testing, which undermines reproducibility and the rigor of the comparisons. Because the empirical coverage is strong but methodological gaps remain, I give a slightly reduced score of 4.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a solid set of established baselines (RNN/RNTN, LSTM/Bi-LSTM, Tree-LSTM, CNN/CNN-Tensor, DAN, NCSL) on standard datasets (MR, SST) using the common accuracy metric, which constitutes good empirical coverage. However, fairness is undermined by missing experimental details (no hyperparameter reporting or code) and no mention of multiple runs/statistical significance, and it is unclear whether the authors acknowledge cases where prior methods tie or outperform their approach; these omissions prevent a fully rigorous and reproducible comparison. Overall the comparisons are good but suffer from methodological gaps that a theoretical reviewer finds important.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a broad set of appropriate and standard baselines (RNN/RNTN, LSTM/Bi-LSTM, Tree-LSTM, CNN variants, DAN, and a neural lexicon-based model NCSL) on standard datasets (MR and SST) using the same metric (accuracy). The authors also discuss related work thoroughly and explicitly situate their contributions relative to lexicon- and syntactic-tree-based methods; differences to baselines are analyzed via component-wise regularizers and reported gains. From an applied-linguistics standpoint this is a comprehensive and fair empirical comparison with the most relevant competing approaches.

main_claims: Proposes a Linguistically Regularized LSTM (LR-LSTM / LR-Bi-LSTM) that incorporates linguistic knowledge (sentiment lexicons, negation, and intensity words) into LSTM-based sentence-level sentiment classification.

Introduces four linguistic regularizers — Non-Sentiment Regularizer (NSR), Sentiment Regularizer (SR), Negation Regularizer (NR), and Intensity Regularizer (IR) — using KL-divergence constraints, shifting distributions, and word-specific transformation matrices to model sentiment drift.

Demonstrates that models trained only with sentence-level annotation can capture the linguistic roles of sentiment words, negation words, and intensity words, and that adding the regularizers improves accuracy (results reported on MR and SST).
_summary.methodology: Augment LSTM / Bidirectional LSTM sentence classifiers with linguistic regularizers: NSR constrains adjacent non-sentiment positions to have similar predicted sentiment distributions; SR applies class-specific shifting distributions for sentiment words; NR and IR use word-specific transformation matrices to model negation and intensity effects; losses use margin-based KL-divergence terms and bidirectional min-distance aggregation.
limitations: Does not consider modification span (negation/intensity scope) — explicitly omitted for simplicity.
Negation scope resolution is beyond the scope of this work.
Negation and intensity regularizers are less impactful overall due to low prevalence in test data (14% sentences contain negation, 23% contain intensity words).

reviewer_a.name: Theoretical Purist
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in machine learning and computational linguistics, with a focus on principled model design and theoretical guarantees for NLP models.
traits: Requires formal/mathematical justification for newly introduced regularizers or guarantees about optimization/convergence; will heavily penalize papers lacking theory for novel loss terms.
Demands comprehensive ablation studies: each proposed component must be individually evaluated and justified; deducts points if any regularizer or hyperparameter interaction is not isolated.
Penalizes reliance on external linguistic resources (e.g., sentiment lexicons) unless there is analysis of domain transfer/generalization and clear motivation for necessity.
Insists on statistical significance testing (e.g., multiple runs with variance) for reported improvements; treats single-run improvements under 1% as inconclusive.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Critical, skeptically examines empirical claims for theoretical grounding and rigorous ablation/significance evidence before accepting incremental methodological contributions.

reviewer_b.name: Applied Linguistic Pragmatist
reviewer_b.background: Senior NLP researcher in industry focusing on applied sentiment analysis and interpretable models that integrate linguistic knowledge for better real-world performance.
traits: Rewards methods that incorporate linguistic priors (lexicons, negation/intensity modeling) into end-to-end neural models, valuing interpretability and practical usefulness.
Prefers clear qualitative analyses (visualizations, case studies) demonstrating that model components capture intended linguistic phenomena; will upscore papers with convincing qualitative evidence.

Values performance gains on standard benchmarks and pragmatic simplicity (models that improve accuracy without complex data requirements); credits methods that learn from sentence-level annotation alone.
Favors reproducibility and practical deployment considerations: gives credit if code/lexicons/hyperparameters are released or if the approach is easily implementable.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic and positive, emphasizes empirical gains, interpretability, and applicability of linguistically informed models for real-world sentiment analysis tasks.
